sections.title: Fats
sections.narration: 55.5g of fat. saturated fat, omega3, and polyunsaturated fat are doing most of the work. for nuts, fat quality has to justify the calorie density.
sections.subtitleText: 55.5g of fat. saturated fat, omega3, and polyunsaturated fat are doing most of the work. for nuts, fat quality has to justify the calorie density.

sections.narration: 18.8g of carbs. fibre is doing a lot of the work here. for a nut, this is mostly about fat quality and support.

sections.title: Proteins
sections.narration: 21.2g of protein. bioavailability and amino acid quality are both strong. protein is support here, not the whole argument.

sections.title: Minerals

sections.title: Pros
sections.narration: on the plus side: still a recognisable whole-food option. that keeps them cleaner than most processed snacks. holds up as a better snack default than most packaged options. that real-world swap value counts. fat and texture help satiety. they can hold up better than many snack foods.

sections.title: Cons
sections.narration: the drawbacks next: spread form makes overdoing it easier. still good, but less self-limiting than whole nuts. portion control can get slippery fast. dense foods punish casual handful math. calorie density is very high. that makes portion control a real issue.

closing.summary: Almond Butter (Unsweetened) does have some solid upside. but the tradeoffs keep it from climbing higher. best in small amounts where the fats and minerals matter more than sheer calories.
